author: @behappymike
text: Hey Robert. Ever since you showed the first 3d printer I've been looking for a reason to get in to 3d printing. Well, I found a problem that needed a solution so I worked my way through tinkercad to get it drawn up. Then I thought that I would try out one of the online printing services that have now spun up only to find my design would be over £120 to get printed which I'm gutted with. Its a cylinder 140mm wide and 40mm tall with a few bits in the middle. May be I've done something wrong, may be I'm totally under estimating the cost of the filament?

replies.author: @DKFX1
replies.text: The cost of filament is not large. About 15 -25 pounds a kilogram. These people you talking about are either using a much to expensive machine to perform a very easy task that could be done on a cheaper machine, or they're simply overcharging.

author: @Manski_123
text: I'm quite sure that the person selling the Dr Who chess set on eBay is infringing several Intellectual Property laws. If we're looking at the same one, they have highlighted in Red that it is "Fan Art" and "Not an officially licenced product" but this declaration does not entitle them to use designs created by and belonging to someone else, for their own commercial gain. Especially if they're selling large volumes, as appears to be happening here. In this case the intellectual property would belong to BBC Worldwide or individual designers that worked for Dr Who. The person who created the 3D files and uploaded them to Thingiverse almost certainly does not own the copyright or trade mark for their "creation". Therefore, obtaining the "correct" (commercial) licence from Thingiverse does not necessarily mean it is legal to sell reproductions (3D Prints). If you think about it , it's basically counterfeiting a popular brand/product. People are buying this particular chess set (at over 70$.. are they mad?) specifically because of its association with Dr Who and because the chess pieces look like the characters from the TV show.  Just a warning to anyone who is considering this as a business, occasionally the owners of trade marks, copyrights etc, will take someone to court as a deterrent to others...it could be you. It may be a murky legal area but to my mind, reproducing someone else's work without acknowledgement or payment is morally suspect. As you may already suspect, I am a designer and sculptor working in Film and TV, I even worked on Dr Who for a while, producing Daleks, Cybermen etc. Sadly, I don't own the rights to any of the characters but I know at least one person who does.

replies.author: @Slaphappy-_-
replies.text: All True! I see this all the time with 3D printing. What’s sad is if you don’t have the ability to openly write files you have bought a tool to only self print others ideas but not supposed to sell.

author: @baldeagle5297
text: One bad thing about files you download is many times they're not manifold. That means they have imperfections that make them impossible to print. Sometimes you can use 3d Builder or one of the other programs that fix the problem, or not. I've had to take many files into Blender and spend a lot of time finding and fixing the problems when the easy ways fail. I happen to enjoy a challenge, but I've given up on more than a few and made my own designs.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

You'll need a print farm to run a business like that.

It's amazing what that trick has become.

author: @StrumHollow
text: Something sounds off in the millions story. Good printers have not been around that long. The guy who sold thousands must have had multiple machines. If old math still works, one printer would take well over 8 years to make 12,000 running 24/7 and the average set on ebay goes for $50 with a lot of competition. At that price, 12000 would gross $600,000 minus the costs to produce, (filament, electric, machines, shipping supplies) fees to sell, shipping costs & taxes and what do ya got?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: your maths is off - I got 4 years

replies.author: @StrumHollow
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Did I forget to carry the 1 ?  12k x 6hrs = 72,000 hrs / 24 = 3,000 days / 365 = 8.21+ yrs. pls, What did I get wrong?

As a joiner/carpenter you and I just have to realise that the industrial revolution is ongoing.  
You see much of the skills I learnt as an apprentice are now outdated with the myriad of new powered tools starting from cordless drills, routers up to CNC machines and beyond.
One thing for sure is I can still do my job when the power goes down, which many younger carpenters couldn't.

replies.author: @jimrogers7841
replies.text:
For over 30 years I sculpted toy prototypes but learned CAD 10 years ago and am the co-owner and designer of Salvinos J R Models, we produce NASCAR model car kits. I have been using resin printers for the last 5 years for testing samples and am still blown away by the quality and immediacy of checking samples of my designs. Resin printers are far more accurate for the details and the prices have become terribly cheap, some less than $200 US; FDM plastic printers aren’t as accurate, but allow for making usable functional plastic parts, and I recently added one to the studio.

Love your videos Robert!

author: @RaperJason
text:
I can honestly say all the 3D printers I have paid for themselves many times over. Learning to use Freecad is a must as you can make so many expensive tools out of ABS and PLA. The press tool to insert bushes into a set of motorcycle forks cost more than the printer I made them on and the molds for the mountain bike suspension parts made a set of £500 forks usable for about £20 instead of £90 
When you can design and print so many bits they quickly pay for themselves and as for fun I would never have forked out the cost of a Storm Trooper Blaster but my printed one looks the part 😁

author: @toml.8210
text:
Like any custom business that doesn't involve mass manufacturing, people will complain about all the little things they don't like, even after you follow their design perfectly, and want something changed 
(a slightly different colour or slightly  taller...)  You will end up losing so much profit, using  this for anything beside your own ideas will drive you out of "business." You'll need many lawyers, and signed liability forms to protect yourself.
But keep on moving forward! The rest of us appreciate it!

author: @newmonengineering
text: I have all 3 types of machines, 3d printers,  cnc machine and laser cutter. Of all of them the laser cutter is co2 and the fastest so it gets the most use but I do use all 3. I love to tinker so they help me build things that I normally wouldn't be able to do precise enough. It also brings out the artist in me. Turns out I can't draw or paint but I can make beautiful things with computer software lol. Any of these machines can be a business. But to do well you will need to get good at designing and know the settings for each machine and build a proper process with check lists. Like every business, it's the process that counts. Without a solid process you will have a very hard time.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest mate - that is very good advice but can I add the caveat that any business requires making a start and sometimes making a start is the best thing to do and learn as you go - so many people just think about doing something and end up never doing it

author: @bikerfirefarter7280
text:
Ooooh! Nice.  I'm thinking UPGRADE project.
How difficult/easy would it be to duplicate the mechanics on a much larger scale, bigger stepper motors, use the output of the Elegoo via interface/drivers, swap the printhead for another type of extruder (fibre-impregnated concrete etc) and start printing garden furniture and archtectural items.  Nice little earner/industry.  Maybe print 'A Cottage'.    (sorry, couldn't resist it).

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice - I think there might be limits - like even heating - it's difficult over a very long distance to maintain alignment even small error magnify over large distances - but interesting idea

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I wasn't thinking thermo-plastic, more of a two component mixture at the extruder/mixer-head, and nozzles of 10-30mm dia, on a rack and pinion system. Also 'blg' stepper motors. There are big systems on the market that print 'houses' but the prices make me faint. In particular I was thinking of printing large 3D murals direct onto existing walls/floors/bridges etc, coloured additives could be injected at the mixer/head as required (for names/signs/logos etc on to things like warehouses/factories/theatres and such). e.g. a giant 3D 'TnT' outside your gaff, or printed/sprayed on the roof for 'google-maps' to see. They do similar on TV'd cricket pitches with dyes, but it requires skilled people.

author: @RTR-MATEY
text: Regardless of what 3D printer ANYONE buys, absolutely *do not* dump $150+ on "Simplify3D" print slicer...I have the "version 4.x" and they "promised" a "version 5" which they recently came out with and it's "half baked"--"Uncle Jessy" YouTube Channel has a review on it. You're much better off using "SuperSlicer" or some other such Cura based slicer as it is open source, much better maintained and FREE!!! Save your money and buy moar filament!!! I highly recommend "PLA+" as it is a good bit more durable than standard PLA. My goto source is Amazon as I can buy a 1 kg roll for around $20, sometimes less on a deal!!!😁👍🏻

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice advice mate the elegoo comes with elegoo Cura as a slicer - it's a Cura port - so you don't even need to get into that

replies.author: @DKFX1
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering It makes sense to switch to the real (ultimaker) Cura as it has more features and is updated frequently.

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: cura is the way to go, most manufacturers have worked with cura and most printers are available in there by default as part of the setup.

author: @seabeepirate
text: I’ve been using an old Creality Ender 3 Pro that I’ve done quite a lot of upgrades to. For me that’s part of the fun of the hobby so it’s a good match. I think of CNC machines including 3D printers as being imagination printers. It’s a game changer for makers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: for sure

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: You guys are giving me encouragement to make the leap.

replies.author: @seabeepirate
replies.text: @justtinkering6713 there’s a bit of a learning curve for sure but I think it’s worth the effort.

author: @CCoburn3
text: For years, I resisted getting a 3D printer. They just weren't useful enough. But that has changed. I recently bought a 3D printer because I need to create an enclosure for a project. And there are now many things on sites like Thingverse that a 3D printer is now worthwhile. I think the best rule is not to print things you can buy for a reasonable price, But there are lots of things that are worth printing.  As for the man who sold so many chess sets, I hope he had sense enough to find a manufacturer. Imagine spending 6 hours per chess set for 15,000 chess sets.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I know - it doesn't bear thinking about lol

replies.author: @DKFX1
replies.text: Don't even think it makes much sense to find a manufacturer these days. Injection molds are really expensive and takes a long time to fabricate. If he guy had 3 printers, had tuned his printer to make a complete set in 5 hours. That's 15 sets a day, selling for 71£, .. Over a thousands pounds in revenue per day or 32 thousands pounds in a month.

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: I think after the prototype stage, couldn't you use the 3d object as a master for a mould? Then just inject or resin mould it

replies.author: @CCoburn3
replies.text:
@DKFX1 But he's already sold 15,000 of them. Do you think his customers will want to hear that they'll get their chess set in 2.73 YEARS? And that is assuming he works 24 hours a day with never a day off and never a hitch in manufacturing. 

I'll agree that as long as you don't go wild like the chess guy, you can have a decent side hustle. But for mass production, you need something more than a few 3D printers.

replies.author: @CCoburn3
replies.text:
@justtinkering6713 For small-scale production that should work. But this guy has sold so many chess sets that I doubt he can handle the manufacturing himself. Plus, I suspect that a manufacturing company could produce the chess sets faster and cheaper than he could. And he wouldn't have to spend all day messing with the molding process. One mistake a lot of small businessmen make is trying to do everything themselves. It sounds like he has a real knack for design and marketing. It would probably be better for him to leave actual manufacturing to someone else.

But I think we can agree that it would be a good problem to have

author: @justtinkering6713
text:
Thanks Rob.
It seems to me that the 3d printer is just half of the formula and cost. You also need to buy all the software and a PC to create the files that the printer needs.
A major expense I would assume. Am I correct on this.?

replies.author: @seabeepirate
replies.text: I use a 7 year old laptop with a 2 core processor to do small modeling in Fusion360 which is free for individuals not using it for business. It’s slow but functional. openSCAD is free, though, it’s less intuitive to use than Fusion360. Parametric design(openSCAD) is a powerful skill to have, but you will probably find paid software is the most efficient route though.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the software came with the printer - yes you need a pc but if you are on the net looking at this chances are you have a pc already lol

replies.author: @TernaryTrout
replies.text: In a phrase, probably not :) Most people following Rob will probably have a PC or laptop.. Suitable software can be had for free...  Freecad for engineering type models, Blender for more 'organic' models... Cura for slicing...  All available on Windows or Linux....

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Thanks guys.
Most of my internet use is by cellphone, its much faster than my windows pc, and isn't tied up for hours, like my pc,  getting microsoft updates that crash it. I wasn't aware that the software was free. Is it any good?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @justtinkering6713 wow - I hardly ever use a phone for the net - I find it too restrictive - for one typing on it is a pain - but each to their own mate - the software is good - certainly all you need to start and for a good while after I would guess - it's all I have used for the past 4 months or so and it does what I need it to
